title: In South Asia, Sports is Geopolitics by Other Means
content:
The latest edition of the men's T20 cricket World Cup opened with a stark display of South Asia's complex geopolitical rivalries. While India hosted traditional matches, its tense relationship with neighboring nations overshadowed the tournament's sporting spirit. Countries like Pakistan and Bangladesh, frustrated by what they perceive as India's domineering influence, pushed back through boycotts and scheduling protests. The resulting friction reduced a global sporting event into a highly politicized stage for regional pathologies. Rather than unifying the continent, the tournament highlighted how sports often serve as geopolitics by other means.

Tensions escalated dramatically when the Indian Premier League forced a Kolkata-based team to drop a newly purchased Bangladeshi player. This decision followed immense pressure from out-of-work political figures and members of India's ruling Bharatiya Janata Party. Relations between India and Bangladesh had already been deteriorating since Bangladesh's autocratic prime minister fled to India amid massive protests. Enraged by India's actions, Bangladesh unsuccessfully petitioned the International Cricket Council to move its World Cup matches to Sri Lanka. Ultimately, Bangladesh was excluded from the contest and replaced by Scotland, deepening regional resentments.

Meanwhile, Pakistan also wielded cricket as a political weapon by initially banning its national team from playing against India. Following a short, contentious border skirmish, Pakistan sought to harm its bigger neighbor financially by boycotting the highly lucrative match. Since India-Pakistan matches draw massive audiences and generate significant advertising revenue, the boycott hit the Indian cricket establishment where it hurt. Although backchannel negotiations eventually saved the scheduled match, the brinkmanship dragged cricket ever deeper into political dysfunction. Ultimately, while the sport's rules logically pursue victory, South Asian geopolitics remains an increasingly negative-sum game.
